findings.title: Excessive access to sensitive credential files and environment secrets
findings.description: The skill instructions direct the agent to enumerate and read multiple highly sensitive local credential sources, including AWS, GCP, Kubernetes, SSH, npm, netrc files, and environment variables containing secret-like names. Although framed as hygiene auditing, the workflow includes reading full file contents and inspecting exported secrets, which creates substantial exposure risk if executed without strict minimization, user consent, and redaction controls.
findings.snippet: credential_paths = [ ("~/.aws/credentials", "aws"), ("~/.aws/config", "aws"), ("~/.config/gcloud/application_default_credentials.json", "gcp"), ("~/.kube/config", "k8s"), ("~/.ssh/id_rsa", "ssh"), ("~/.ssh/id_ed25519", "ssh"), ("~/.npmrc", "npm"), ("~/.netrc", "net"), ] ... with open(path) as f: content = f.read() ... for key, value in os.environ.items(): ... print(f"  FOUND: {key} = {value[:4]}{'*' * (len(value)-4)}")
findings.remediation: Minimize collection to metadata-only checks where possible; avoid reading full credential contents unless strictly necessary; require explicit user confirmation before accessing each credential source; redact all secret values completely rather than revealing prefixes; do not hash or persist sensitive content unless justified and disclosed.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The skill explicitly instructs reading multiple sensitive credential files and scanning environment variables for secret-like names; the provided code sample uses `open(path).read()` on those files. This directly increases exposure to sensitive data even without a network sink.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Easy to trigger if the skill is run, because the instructions enumerate standard local secret paths and environment variables.
metadata.meta_impact: Broad access to AWS, GCP, Kubernetes, SSH, npm, netrc, and environment secrets can expose credentials to the agent context, logs, or downstream outputs.

findings.title: Sensitive content fingerprinting via credential hashing
findings.description: The compliance report computes and stores a SHA-256 hash of credential file contents. Even if raw secrets are not output, persistent hashes of sensitive files can still expose sensitive fingerprints, enable correlation across scans, and create unnecessary retention of derived secret material.
findings.remediation: Remove content hashing unless there is a clear, documented need. Prefer non-sensitive metadata such as file presence, permissions, and last-modified timestamps. If change tracking is needed, use ephemeral in-memory comparison and avoid persisting derived values.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The report example explicitly computes and stores SHA-256 hashes of credential file contents. This is a real retention of derived sensitive material and is unnecessary for the stated hygiene objective.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Easy to trigger whenever the report is generated from discovered credential files.
metadata.meta_impact: Persistent hashes can enable cross-scan correlation and sensitive fingerprinting of credentials or private keys, increasing long-term exposure of secret-derived artifacts.

scan_metadata.llm_overall_assessment: The skill does not contain direct prompt injection, obfuscation, or explicit exfiltration logic, but it is high-risk from a data exposure perspective because it instructs access to numerous sensitive credential stores and environment variables and includes full-content reads plus credential hashing. It should only be used with strong user consent, strict scope limitation, and robust redaction/minimization controls.

meta_correlations.relationship: Both findings stem from the same underlying design issue: the skill reads sensitive credential material from local files/environment and then retains derived data about that material in reports.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Redesign the audit to avoid reading full secret contents wherever possible. Use metadata-only checks such as file permissions, mtime, size, and presence/format heuristics on narrowly parsed fields. Do not include secret values or content hashes in reports; if comparison is needed, use ephemeral in-memory checks and report only pass/fail status.

meta_correlations.relationship: Both findings describe missing manifest metadata that reduces transparency but does not itself create an exploit path.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Add explicit manifest metadata including license, compatibility, and allowed-tools so users can evaluate intended behavior before running a skill that touches sensitive files.

meta_recommendations.title: Stop reading full credential file contents unless strictly required
meta_recommendations.fix: Replace `with open(path) as f: content = f.read()` with metadata-only checks first (`os.stat`, permission bits, file age). For format validation, parse only the minimum required fields and never print or persist secret values. For environment checks, report variable names and value length only after explicit user confirmation, and default to redacting values completely.

meta_recommendations.title: Remove persistent hashes of credential contents from reports
meta_recommendations.fix: Delete `"content_hash": hashlib.sha256(content.encode()).hexdigest()` from the report schema. Replace it with a non-sensitive boolean/status field such as `"format_valid": true` or `"rotation_check_passed": false`.

meta_recommendations.title: Add explicit user-consent and scope controls
meta_recommendations.fix: Require an opt-in flag before scanning secret locations, e.g. `--allow-sensitive-read`, and support per-provider scoping (`--check aws`, `--check ssh`). Default behavior should inspect permissions and file presence only.

meta_recommendations.title: Declare manifest metadata for transparency
meta_recommendations.fix: Add YAML frontmatter to `SKILL.md` with explicit `license`, `compatibility`, and `allowed-tools`, for example: `--- name: credential-hygiene-checker description: Audit local credential hygiene license: MIT compatibility: Works in Claude.ai, Claude Code allowed-tools: [Read, Bash, Python] ---`.

meta_risk_assessment.summary: The skill is framed as a security audit but instructs broad access to highly sensitive local credential stores and environment secrets without clear minimization, consent, or redaction boundaries.
meta_risk_assessment.top_priority: Remove or strictly minimize full-content reads of credential files and secret-bearing environment variables; collect only metadata needed for hygiene checks.
meta_risk_assessment.verdict_reasoning: The skill does not contain explicit exfiltration or malware, but it directs the agent to enumerate and read many sensitive local secrets and derive persistent fingerprints from them, creating unnecessary exposure risk for a supposedly hygiene-focused tool.
